hook: You already know how to prove things directly — you start with what's given and build a chain of logic to your conclusion. But what if the direct path is invisible? Consider this: how would you prove that there is no largest prime number, when you can't possibly check every number? There's no formula, no direct construction — yet mathematicians are completely certain. How can you prove something is impossible?
concept:
Proof by contradiction works by assuming the opposite of what you want to prove, then showing that assumption leads to a logical impossibility. Formally: to prove a statement $P$, we assume $
eg P$ (not $P$) is true, and derive a contradiction — a statement of the form $Q \wedge 
eg Q$ for some proposition $Q$. Since a contradiction cannot be true, our assumption $
eg P$ must be false, which means $P$ must be true.
anatomy:
A proof by contradiction has four structural parts. The negated assumption is the explicit statement $
eg P$ that you declare true at the outset — this is the engine of the whole argument. The deductive chain is the sequence of valid logical steps you draw from $
eg P$, combined with known facts and axioms. The contradiction is the specific impossible statement you arrive at, typically written as $Q \wedge 
eg Q$ or as two conclusions that directly conflict with each other. Finally, the conclusion is the closing statement asserting that since $
eg P$ leads to a contradiction, $P$ must be true.

worked_example.description_latex: We state the claim we want to prove. Because irrationality is defined as the negation of rationality, this is a perfect candidate for contradiction — we will assume the opposite and see what breaks.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Assume } \sqrt{2} \text{ is rational, so } \sqrt{2} = \frac{p}{q} \text{ where } p, q \in \mathbb{Z},\, q \neq 0,\, \gcd(p,q)=1.
worked_example.description_latex: This is our negated assumption — $\neg P$. We assume $\sqrt{2}$ IS rational, meaning it can be written as a fraction in lowest terms. The condition $\gcd(p,q)=1$ is crucial: it encodes that the fraction is fully reduced, which we will contradict.
We square both sides and rearrange to get $p^2 = 2q^2$, which tells us $p^2$ is even. Since 2 is prime, if $2 \mid p^2$ then $2 \mid p$, so we can write $p = 2k$ for some integer $k$.
Substituting $p = 2k$ back into $p^2 = 2q^2$ gives $q^2 = 2k^2$, which means $q^2$ is also even, and by the same reasoning $2 \mid q$. Now we have derived that both $p$ and $q$ are divisible by 2.

worked_example.expression_latex: 2 \mid p \text{ and } 2 \mid q \implies \gcd(p,q) \geq 2. \text{ But } \gcd(p,q)=1. \text{ Contradiction. } \therefore \sqrt{2} \text{ is irrational.}
worked_example.description_latex: This is the contradiction: we assumed $\gcd(p,q)=1$ but derived $\gcd(p,q) \geq 2$ — both cannot be true simultaneously. Since our only unjustified assumption was that $\sqrt{2}$ is rational, that assumption must be false, and we conclude $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Claim: There is no largest even integer.}
partial_example.description_latex: We want to prove that no single even integer is greater than all others. A direct proof seems hard because we'd have to somehow account for every even number, so contradiction is a natural approach here.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Assume there exists a largest even integer } n, \text{ so } n \geq m \text{ for every even integer } m.
partial_example.description_latex: This is the negated assumption: we suppose the claim is FALSE and that some specific even integer $n$ is the largest. All of our subsequent work flows from this assumption.

Now construct an even integer strictly greater than $n$ using $n$ itself, and explain why this contradicts the assumption that $n$ is the largest even integer. (Hint: what happens if you add 2 to $n$?)

partial_example.expression_latex: n+2 > n \text{ and } n+2 \text{ is even. This contradicts } n \text{ being the largest even integer. } \therefore \text{ no largest even integer exists.}
partial_example.description_latex: The contradiction is clean: $n+2$ is an even integer (the sum of two even numbers) and it is strictly larger than $n$, which directly contradicts our assumption. This closes the proof.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Prove by contradiction: there is no smallest positive rational number.}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Assume } r > 0 \text{ is the smallest positive rational. Then } r/2 > 0 \text{ is also a positive rational and } r/2 < r, \text{ contradicting minimality. } \square

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Prove by contradiction: if } n^2 \text{ is even, then } n \text{ is even.}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Assume } n \text{ is odd, so } n = 2k+1. \text{ Then } n^2 = 4k^2+4k+1 = 2(2k^2+2k)+1, \text{ which is odd. This contradicts } n^2 \text{ being even.} \square
\text{Assume } \log_2 3 = p/q \text{ with } p,q \in \mathbb{Z}^+. \text{ Then } 2^{p/q}=3 \implies 2^p = 3^q. \text{ But } 2^p \text{ is even and } 3^q \text{ is odd — contradiction.} \square

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Prove by contradiction: if } p \text{ and } q \text{ are distinct primes, then } \sqrt{pq} \text{ is irrational.}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Assume } \sqrt{pq} = a/b \text{ in lowest terms. Then } pq \cdot b^2 = a^2, \text{ so } p \mid a^2 \implies p \mid a. \text{ Write } a=pk; \text{ then } q b^2 = p k^2, \text{ so } p \mid b^2 \implies p \mid b, \text{ contradicting } \gcd(a,b)=1. \square

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A room contains 13 people. Prove by contradiction that at least two of them share a birth month.}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Assume no two people share a birth month. Then the 13 people occupy 13 distinct months. But there are only 12 months — a contradiction. Therefore at least two share a birth month.} \square

common_mistakes: Students write 'assume P is false' but never explicitly state what $\neg P$ looks like in concrete terms, making the deductive chain vague. Fix: always translate $\neg P$ into a specific mathematical statement (e.g., 'assume $\sqrt{2} = p/q$') before proceeding.

Students reach a statement that seems 'weird' or unlikely and declare a contradiction without identifying the specific conflicting pair of statements. Fix: always name the contradiction explicitly — state both the thing you assumed and the thing you derived that directly opposes it.

Students confuse proof by contradiction with proof by contrapositive: they try to prove $
eg Q \implies 
eg P$ but label it a contradiction proof. Fix: remember that contradiction assumes $
eg P$ AND $Q$ simultaneously (the full claim and its negation) and derives any logical falsehood, whereas contrapositive is a separate direct-proof strategy.
untested_variants: Proving that the set of primes is infinite (Euclid's theorem), which requires constructing a product of known primes plus 1 and arguing about its prime factors.
Contradiction proofs involving graph theory, such as proving that a graph with $n$ vertices and more than $\binom{n-1}{2}$ edges must be connected.
Proving irrationality of numbers like $\sqrt[3]{2}$ or $2^{1/n}$ for $n \geq 3$, which require modular arithmetic arguments beyond the basic even/odd structure used in the $\sqrt{2}$ proof.
Using contradiction within a combinatorial existence argument, such as proving that any sequence of $n^2+1$ distinct integers contains a monotone subsequence of length $n+1$ (Erdős–Szekeres theorem).
